title: Descriptive Statistics
learning_objectives: Compute mean, median, mode, and range.
Use sum = mean × count to handle 'add/remove a value' and 'missing value' problems.
Reason about standard deviation as spread about the mean (no formula needed on the GMAT).
Compare measures of center for skewed data.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Addition, division, and putting numbers in order.

items.prompt: If you add a value equal to the current mean, does the mean change?
items.answer: No — adding the mean itself leaves the average unchanged.
items.targets: how the mean responds to new data

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves the four basic measures and the key intuition (the mean is a balance point) that powers today's sum-based reasoning.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If median is shaky with an even count, remind them to sort first and average the two middle values.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You computed means by adding then dividing. The GMAT rarely asks that directly — it gives you the average and asks you to work backward. The move is to flip the formula into sum = mean × count. Q5's insight (the mean is a balance point) is why that flip works, and it's the key to every 'missing value' and 'new average' question today.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can compute mean/median/mode/range and use sum = mean × count to solve missing-value and changing-average problems, and reason qualitatively about standard deviation.
success_criteria: I can convert between an average and a total using count.
I can find a missing value given an average.
I can say which of two data sets has the larger spread and why.

opening_script.move: Bridge: flip mean = sum/count into sum = mean × count.
I do: new average after adding a value.

i_do.stem: The average (arithmetic mean) of 5 numbers is 12. If a sixth number, 30, is added to the set, what is the new average?
i_do.explanation: Sum = mean × count = 12 × 5 = 60. New sum = 60 + 30 = 90 over 6 numbers, so the new average = 90/6 = 15.
think_aloud_steps: Convert the average to a sum: 12 × 5 = 60.
i_do.key_takeaway: Don't average the averages — turn the mean into a SUM (mean × count), adjust, then divide by the new count.

we_do.explanation: Sort: 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9. With 6 values the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th: (4 + 6)/2 = 5.
scaffold_hints: Always sort before finding a median.
Six values → no single middle; use the two middle ones.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Sort first, then average the two middle values for an even-sized list.

we_do.stem: The mean of 6 numbers is 10. What is their sum?

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Multiply the mean by how many numbers there are.

you_do.stem: The average of 8 and x is 14. What is x?
For the set {3, 5, 5, 9, 13}, which is the largest: the mean, the median, or the mode?
you_do.explanation: Mean = 35/5 = 7; median = 5 (middle); mode = 5 (most frequent). The mean, 7, is largest.

you_do.stem: The average of 5 test scores is 82. Four of the scores are 78, 85, 90, and 75. What is the fifth score?
you_do.explanation: Total = 82 × 5 = 410. The four given sum to 328, so the fifth = 410 − 328 = 82.

you_do.stem: Which set has the greater standard deviation?  P = {10, 10, 10, 10};  Q = {7, 9, 11, 13}.
you_do.explanation: Standard deviation measures spread about the mean. P has no spread (SD = 0); Q's values vary about their mean, so Q has the greater SD.

sample_held_out_items.stem: The average of 4 numbers is 9. After one number is removed, the average of the remaining 3 is 8. What number was removed?

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.

citations.name: OpenStax, Introductory Statistics — Measures of center and spread
citations.note: Mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation.

citations.name: Khan Academy — Summarizing quantitative data
citations.note: Center, spread, and effects of changing data.
